titleIntro.speaker: Scene
titleIntro.text: [Red Cross logo fades in. Office ambience. A slow zoom on an empty meeting room.]

titleIntro.speaker: Narrator
titleIntro.text: In this building, words can build careers.

titleIntro.speaker: Narrator
titleIntro.text: Every day, someone has to speak. And someone has to listen.

manager.speaker: Narrator
manager.text: Welcome, Manager. Your name is Alex.

manager.speaker: Narrator
manager.text: You lead a team at the Singapore Red Cross.

manager.speaker: Narrator
manager.text: Your people are passionate. But feedback conversations? Those are never easy.

manager.speaker: Narrator
manager.text: Let's see how you handle them.

employee.speaker: Narrator
employee.text: You're a coordinator at the Singapore Red Cross.

employee.speaker: Narrator
employee.text: You care deeply about this work. But feedback? It doesn't always land the way it's meant to.

employee.speaker: Narrator
employee.text: Let's see how you handle it.

gameEnd.speaker: Scene
gameEnd.text: [Office fades. Both characters — Alex and Jamie — visible in the background, mid-conversation.]

gameEnd.speaker: Narrator
gameEnd.text: Every conversation at the Red Cross shapes the culture around you.

gameEnd.speaker: Narrator
gameEnd.text: REAL or CARE — these aren't just frameworks.

gameEnd.speaker: Narrator
gameEnd.text: They're choices. Made in small moments.

gameEnd.speaker: Narrator
gameEnd.text: Play again — try the other side.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: A small meeting room. Alex sits across from JAMIE, a dedicated but recently tardy team member.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Jamie has been arriving 15–20 minutes late almost every day this week.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: But today, Jamie also submitted the best community outreach report your team has seen in months.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: It's time for the feedback conversation. You take a breath.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Jamie, thanks for coming in. I wanted to catch up on a few things.

intro.speaker: Jamie
intro.text: Sure. Is everything okay?

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [What do you open with?]

recognise-specific-work.label: A
recognise-specific-work.text: "Your report this week was genuinely impressive. The way you broke down the volunteer data — I want us to use that format going forward."

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: (visibly relaxing) Oh. Thank you. I put a lot of work into that.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: It shows. And because I value that kind of work, I also want to address something. You've been coming in late most days. I've noticed, and I want to understand what's going on.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: I'm sorry. The MRT's been delayed and I haven't sorted out an earlier route yet.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: That's helpful to know. Let's figure out what we can do.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Jamie nods. The air in the room is open — not tense.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — R: RECOGNISE You demonstrated RECOGNISE. By opening with specific, observable praise before raising the issue, you created psychological safety. Jamie felt seen — not ambushed. Key: Shine a spotlight on what was done well first. Be specific. Then address the gap.

lead-with-lateness.label: B
lead-with-lateness.text: "Let's get straight to it — you've been late every single day this week."

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: (stiffening) I — yes, I know. But I've had transport issues and I've still been getting my work done.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: That's not the point. The lateness is a problem.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: (flatly) Okay.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [The report on the table goes unmentioned. Jamie leaves only knowing what they did wrong — not what they did right.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: R: RECOGNISE You skipped RECOGNISE. Jumping straight to the problem activated Jamie's identity trigger. They felt judged, not coached. And you missed a chance to build trust. What to try: Acknowledge specific strengths before raising concerns. It changes everything.

vague-check-in.label: C
vague-check-in.text: "I just wanted to check in generally. How are you feeling about your workload lately?"

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: I'm okay. Just a bit stretched.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: I see. I also wanted to bring up the punctuality — you've been late a lot this week.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: Yeah. I'll try to do better.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [The feedback was given. But it felt like a box was ticked, not a conversation.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: △ FEEDBACK CARD — PARTIAL: R: RECOGNISE You were kind, but you missed RECOGNISE. Opening vaguely meant the conversation lost direction early. Jamie wasn't made to feel seen. Try: Shine a specific spotlight on a real achievement first. Vague check-ins don't build trust.

real-uneven-scale.title: The Uneven Scale

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Alex reviews mid-year evaluations for two team coordinators — SAM and JORDAN. Both are strong performers, but in different ways.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Sam is methodical, quiet, and hits every KPI.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Jordan is energetic, deeply loved by the team — and missed two deadlines last month after going above and beyond at the relief drive.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: You meet with Jordan first.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Jordan, you were incredible during the relief drive. The team really rallied around you.

intro.speaker: Jordan
intro.text: Thank you. That week meant a lot to me.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: There's also the matter of the two missed deadlines.

intro.speaker: Jordan
intro.text: (pausing) I know. I got too caught up in the drive.

real-uneven-scale.decisionPrompt: How do you evaluate both the relief-drive impact and the missed deadlines?

set-deadlines-aside.label: A
set-deadlines-aside.text: "Given what you achieved that week, I'm going to set those aside for this review. The impact was worth it."

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You file the review with glowing remarks. The missed deadlines are barely a footnote.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Weeks later, Sam hears through the team that Jordan's review was near-perfect.

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Sam says nothing. But something quietly shifts.

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: E: EVALUATE You skipped EVALUATE. Evaluating on enthusiasm over evidence isn't fair — to Jordan or to Sam. EVALUATE means: weigh contributions, behaviours, and outcomes. Not feelings about the person.

balance-both-outcomes.label: B
balance-both-outcomes.text: "I have to evaluate you on both. The energy was brilliant. The missed deadlines affected the team's planning cycle. Both things are real."

outcome.speaker: Jordan
outcome.text: (nodding slowly) That's fair. I didn't think about how it affected the planning team downstream.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: That awareness matters. I want to see you grow in both — the drive you have and the structure the role needs.

outcome.speaker: Jordan
outcome.text: I appreciate the honesty.

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — E: EVALUATE You demonstrated EVALUATE. You balanced recognition with accountability. That's how you build a consistent, fair team culture. Key: Weigh observable outcomes — not how much you like someone.

label-admin-weakness.label: C
label-admin-weakness.text: "You're a people person, Jordan. The admin side just might not be your strength."

outcome.speaker: Jordan
outcome.text: (quietly) Oh. I didn't realise you saw it that way.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You meant it as insight. Jordan heard: "I'm not capable of the full role."]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: E: EVALUATE You activated an identity trigger. Labelling someone's "type" shifts from behaviour to character. That shuts people down. EVALUATE means: assess what was done — not who you think they are.

real-quiet-one.title: The Quiet One

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Weekly check-in. Alex meets with PRIYA, a newer staff member who produces good work but never speaks in team meetings.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Priya is solid individually. But in group settings, she disappears.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Other coordinators are starting to notice.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Priya, you produce solid work — that's always been clear. But I've noticed you tend to stay quiet in our team meetings.

intro.speaker: Priya
intro.text: (tense) I don't always feel like I have something worth saying.

real-quiet-one.decisionPrompt: How do you respond to Priya?

tell-priya-speak-up.label: A
tell-priya-speak-up.text: "You need to speak up more. Other people are noticing the silence."

outcome.speaker: Priya
outcome.text: (quietly) I'll try.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [She nods. But that's not agreement — that's shutdown.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: You named the gap. But you gave her no path forward.

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: A: ADVICE You skipped ADVICE. Telling someone what to do without a how is pressure, not guidance. It increases anxiety, not confidence. Try: Act as a navigator. Ask first. Then offer practical, specific steps.

ask-what-is-hard.label: B
ask-what-is-hard.text: "What makes it hard to share in those moments?"

outcome.speaker: Priya
outcome.text: (pausing) I guess... I worry my ideas aren't as polished as everyone else's.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: That's really helpful to hear. Half-formed ideas are often where the best thinking starts. Can we try something? I'll give you a heads-up on one agenda item before each meeting.

outcome.speaker: Priya
outcome.text: I'd like that.

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — A: ADVICE You demonstrated ADVICE. You asked before you advised. Then you offered a practical, low-pressure next step. Key: Reflective questions + actionable suggestions = real navigation, not just direction.

offer-one-idea-plan.label: C
offer-one-idea-plan.text: "Here's a small idea — before each meeting, write down just one thing you want to say. I'll also make sure to create space for you."

outcome.speaker: Priya
outcome.text: That... actually sounds manageable.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Let's try it for two weeks and check in.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Priya leaves looking lighter.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — A: ADVICE You demonstrated ADVICE. Giving someone a small, specific action respects their pace. That's the difference between coaching and commanding. Key: Make the path forward concrete and kind.

real-star-stopped-caring.title: The Star Who Stopped Caring

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Year-end review with MARCUS, your highest-performing community officer. His numbers are excellent — but lately, something is off.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Marcus has been here three years. Best numbers in the team.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: But recently he's been quiet in planning sessions. And he turned down a chance to mentor a junior officer.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Marcus, your work this year has been outstanding. Three major campaigns. Highest volunteer satisfaction scores on the team.

intro.speaker: Marcus
intro.text: (flatly) Thanks.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: I also noticed you stepped back from the mentoring opportunity. I was a bit surprised.

intro.speaker: Marcus
intro.text: I just didn't see the point.

real-star-stopped-caring.decisionPrompt: How do you reconnect Marcus with purpose?

mentoring-is-expected.label: A
mentoring-is-expected.text: "That's disappointing. Mentoring is expected at your level."

outcome.speaker: Marcus
outcome.text: (flatly) Okay.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [He agrees. But nothing changes. He doesn't know why it matters — only that you expect it.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: L: LINK You skipped LINK. Compliance without meaning doesn't last. Marcus isn't resistant — he's disconnected. LINK means: show people how their actions today shape their future and the team's future.

connect-to-leadership.label: B
connect-to-leadership.text: "I hear that. Can I share what I see when I think about where you could be heading?"

outcome.speaker: Marcus
outcome.text: (slightly curious) What do you mean?

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: I think you have the instinct to lead at a higher level. When you mentor someone, you're not just helping one person — you're shaping how the next generation of this team thinks about the mission.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Your experience is rare here. I'd hate for it to stay in one lane.

outcome.speaker: Marcus
outcome.text: (quietly) I hadn't thought about it like that.

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — L: LINK You demonstrated LINK. You connected Marcus's behaviour to something bigger — his impact and future potential. That's what reignites purpose. Key: Build a bridge between today's choices and tomorrow's growth. Always.

move-to-targets.label: C
move-to-targets.text: "Understood. Let's move on to next year's targets."

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You move on. The moment passes. Marcus leaves the review feeling exactly the same.]

feedback.speaker: REAL Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: L: LINK You skipped LINK. Feedback that ends at correction doesn't move people. It just informs them. Try: Close every feedback conversation by connecting it to future impact and growth. That's what makes it stick.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: End of a long, brutal week. Your manager Alex catches you in the corridor just before you leave.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Understaffed. Late nights. A volunteer event that almost fell apart. You are exhausted.

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Jamie, quick word. I've been hearing from a few team members that you've been a bit dismissive in the morning briefings. Some people feel their ideas aren't being heard.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Your chest tightens. You've been holding this team together all week.]

react-from-exhaustion.label: A
react-from-exhaustion.text: (frustrated) "Are you serious? I've been the only one holding things together this entire week."

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: (taken aback) I understand you've been under pressure, but—

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: No. I don't think you do.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [The conversation derails. Nothing gets resolved. You walk away feeling vindicated — and somehow, worse.]

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: C: COMPOSE You skipped COMPOSE. Reacting from exhaustion hijacked the conversation. The feedback got buried under the emotional reaction. COMPOSE means: pause before responding. Treat feedback as a diagnostic report — not a personal verdict.

ask-for-a-moment.label: B
ask-for-a-moment.text: (quietly) "Okay. Can I have a moment? I want to make sure I actually hear this properly."

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Of course. Take your time.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Jamie takes a breath. Internal monologue:]

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: (to self) I'm tired. I might be hearing this wrong. This isn't an attack. Just... listen.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: Okay. I'm ready. Can you walk me through what was shared?

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: A few people said they raised ideas and felt you moved past them without acknowledging them.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: I wasn't aware it came across that way. Can we talk more tomorrow so I can sit with it tonight?

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — C: COMPOSE You demonstrated COMPOSE. You paused, reframed internally, and responded with calm curiosity — not defensiveness. That's emotional regulation in real time. Key: Pause. Breathe. The feedback is data, not a verdict.

promise-quick-fix.label: C
promise-quick-fix.text: "That's fine. I'll fix it." (turns to leave)

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You leave quickly. The words echo in your head the whole way home.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Did you handle that well? Or did you just escape it?

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: △ FEEDBACK CARD — PARTIAL: C: COMPOSE You stayed calm — but you deflected. Saying "I'll fix it" without understanding the feedback means nothing actually changes. COMPOSE isn't just staying quiet. It's being present enough to actually receive the message.

care-rating-stings.title: The Rating That Stings

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Mid-year review. Alex shares your rating — Meets Expectations. You were certain it would be Exceeds.]

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Overall, your performance this cycle is solid. Meets Expectations.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [The words land hard. Late nights. Covered shifts. Delivered every project.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Your mind races: Is this because of that disagreement last month? Does Alex just not see my effort?

care-rating-stings.decisionPrompt: How do you respond to the rating?

ask-for-criteria.label: A
ask-for-criteria.text: "I'm honestly confused. I feel like I've exceeded expectations in a few areas. Can you walk me through the criteria?"

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Of course. The rating reflects output consistency, stakeholder feedback, and project milestones. You hit most targets — the gap was in the stakeholder communications area.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: I wasn't aware that was weighted as heavily. Can I see the breakdown?

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Absolutely.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You leave with clarity, not just feelings.]

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — A: ANALYZE You demonstrated ANALYZE. You separated your emotional reaction from the actual content. You asked for evidence, not validation. Key: Focus on what was said, not who said it or why you think they said it.

call-rating-personal.label: B
call-rating-personal.text: "I think this rating is unfair. It feels personal."

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: It's not personal, Jamie. The criteria—

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: I know what the criteria say. This just doesn't feel right.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [The conversation becomes about feelings, not facts. No clarity is reached.]

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: A: ANALYZE You activated the Relationship Trigger. When feedback feels like it's about the relationship, you stop hearing the content. ANALYZE means: separate emotional reaction from intent. Focus on behaviours and outcomes — not assumed motivations.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You accept it. The frustration quietly sits in you for weeks.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: You don't ask. You don't understand. And the same gaps stay invisible to you.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: A: ANALYZE Silence isn't acceptance — it's avoidance. Not analyzing the feedback means you lose the only chance to grow from it. ANALYZE means: examine the content, separate fact from perspective, and stay curious.

care-what-did-that-mean.title: What Did That Mean?

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Team debrief after a community health event. Alex gives general feedback to the group — but some of it clearly lands in your direction.]

intro.speaker: Alex
intro.text: Some of the communications during the event could have been clearer. We need to do better on coordination next time.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [You managed the volunteer communications. You're fairly sure that's about you — but you're not sure what exactly went wrong.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: People start packing up. Do you say something?

care-what-did-that-mean.decisionPrompt: People start packing up. Do you say something?

assume-and-work-harder.label: A
assume-and-work-harder.text: (to yourself) "That was definitely about me. I'll just work harder next time." \

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You go home. You replay every moment of the event. You still can't figure out what you did wrong.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Next event. Same issue. Because nothing was ever clarified.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: R: RESOLVE You skipped RESOLVE. Assuming without asking means you try to fix the wrong thing — or nothing at all. RESOLVE means: clarify respectfully. You can't improve what you don't understand.

ask-privately.label: B
ask-privately.text: "Alex, before we wrap up — can I check in privately? I want to make sure I understand the communications feedback."

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Of course. Thanks for asking.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Later, in private:]

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: The volunteer check-in messages went out two hours late. A few people showed up to the wrong station.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: Oh. I didn't realise the timing caused that downstream. I thought the delay was absorbed on the other end.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Now you know. A quick confirmation message 30 minutes before the event would help a lot.

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: That's really clear. Thank you.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — R: RESOLVE You demonstrated RESOLVE. Asking privately and specifically gave you what you needed to actually improve — not just a vague goal to "do better." Key: Clarify without assuming negative intent. Questions build bridges.

challenge-in-public.label: C
challenge-in-public.text: (louder than intended) "Was that directed at me? Because I think the issues came from the last-minute changes, not my end."

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: (caught off guard) It wasn't only about you, Jamie—

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [The room goes quiet. A few colleagues glance over.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: You made your point. But the conversation is now uncomfortable for everyone.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: △ FEEDBACK CARD — PARTIAL: R: RESOLVE You asked — but how you asked matters. Public defensiveness creates tension rather than resolution. RESOLVE works best when done privately and respectfully. The what and the how both count.

intro.speaker: Scene
intro.text: [Scene: Two days after your review. Alex gave you clear feedback — your written reports are detailed but hard to skim. You have a report due in three weeks.]

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: The feedback was fair. You know it.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: Three weeks feels like forever — and also like no time at all.

intro.speaker: Narrator
intro.text: What do you do?

overhaul-everything.label: A
overhaul-everything.text: Spend the first week overhauling your writing style from scratch. New format. New structure. All of it.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Week one: articles on business writing. Week two: three new formats tested.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: The report comes together. But it feels forced. Not quite you.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: Better — but not quite there yet.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: E: EXECUTE Big leaps without structure rarely stick. Trying to change everything at once is overwhelming and rarely leads to real growth. EXECUTE means: break it down. Small, specific, trackable steps — not full transformations.

example-two-changes-checkin.label: B
example-two-changes-checkin.text: Ask Alex for one example of a well-structured report. Pick two specific things to change. Schedule a short check-in in week two.

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: (sharing a report) Here's one that worked well. See how each section opens with the key point?

outcome.speaker: Jamie
outcome.text: Got it. I'll focus on leading with the main finding, and I'll cut my executive summaries down to three lines.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [Week two check-in.]

outcome.speaker: Alex
outcome.text: (reviewing the draft) This is noticeably cleaner. Good work.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You adjusted. You executed. And this time — it stuck.]

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ★ FEEDBACK CARD — E: EXECUTE You demonstrated EXECUTE. You broke the feedback into manageable steps, got a reference point, and checked progress before the deadline. Key: Small steps, followed through. That's how feedback becomes real growth.

adapt-on-the-fly.label: C
adapt-on-the-fly.text: Wait to see what the report needs to be, then adapt your style on the fly.

outcome.speaker: Scene
outcome.text: [You adapt on the fly. The report turns out okay.]

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Three months later, Alex raises the same feedback again.

outcome.speaker: Narrator
outcome.text: Because nothing was really changed. Just adjusted for one report.

feedback.speaker: CARE Coach
feedback.text: ✗ FEEDBACK CARD — MISSED: E: EXECUTE Adapting for one moment isn't execution — it's survival. EXECUTE means making feedback part of how you work, not just how you perform under pressure. Try: Plan ahead. Reflect. Follow up. Build the habit, not just the output.
